narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70 and a family from the middle income bracket, you've put real effort into building a strong academic foundation, including planning to attend law school full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 47% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is unfortunately high enough to tip the scales toward rejecting your application—our goal is to select students with the strongest likelihood of bar success to ensure everyone thrives. This prediction wasn't random; it weighed several key aspects of your profile against patterns from thousands of past students. Your undergraduate GPA stood out as the most influential positive factor, as it's notably higher than the typical 3.21 average, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass the bar. However, your LSAT score of 27 was the top concern, well below the average of about 37, and it significantly pulled the prediction toward anticipating failure. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, lower than the usual 5.5-ish, added to that risk by suggesting some challenges in later undergrad performance compared to peers. Being full-time, like nearly all applicants, helped a bit in the model's eyes, aligning with successful students, while your middle-income background had a minor nudge toward higher risk. Your Year 1 decile of 4 was about average and didn't sway things much. Overall, compared to typical admitted students who pass the bar, your strengths in GPA shine, but the lower LSAT and Year 3 decile made you appear riskier than the safer profiles we prioritize. I know this rejection stings, especially with your impressive GPA showing real potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the average—many students in your position do and get strong results afterward. You might also reflect on what impacted your Year 3 performance and seek targeted prep, like bar-focused tutoring, to build confidence. We're rooting for you; persistence like yours often leads to success in law. Keep pushing, and reapply stronger.
